2. Change the Reqmts Class key and add a short text. (To change the key, use a number
range starting with Z or 9.)
3. Under Assembly, enter your Assembly Type (e.g., “2”).
4. Enter the Order Type (e.g., “PP10”). Change this field for production and process
orders; leave it blank for planned orders.
5. Select Avail. Components if desired.
6. Enter the Dialog Assembly (e.g., “3”). This entry determines the reaction on the ATP
check.
7. Enter a capacity check if desired. Save the entries.

sections.title: Creating a Requirements Type and Adding the Requirements Class
sections.text:
12.7.2 Creating a Requirements Type and Adding the Requirements Class
In this configuration step, you go to menu path SAP IMG  Production  Master Planning 
Demand Management  Planned Independent Requirements  Requirements Types/
Requirements Classes and follow these instructions:
1. Select the Define Requirements Types and Allocate Requirements Class line.
2. Select an existing requirements type line (in this example, RqTy KMFA) and make a
copy of it.
3. Change the RqTy key. Use a number range starting with “Z” or “9” (e.g., change
“KMFA” to “ZMFA”).
4. Enter a short text for Requirements Type.
5. Under ReqCl, enter the new requirements class you created in the previous section.
This step links the requirements type to the requirements class. Save the entries.

sections.title: Creating a Planning Strategy
sections.text:
12.7.3 Creating a Planning Strategy
In the step, you go to menu path SAP IMG  Production  Master Planning  Demand
Management  Planned Independent Requirements  Planning Strategy and follow
these steps:
1. Select the line Define Strategy.
2. Select an existing line (e.g., Strategy 82) and create a copy by clicking Copy As.
3. Change the Strategy key (use a number range starting with Z or 9) and add a short
text.
4. Link this strategy to the newly created requirements types from the previous step.
Save the entries.
© 

655
12.7 Creating a Custom Requirement Class
Tips and Tricks
To link newly created requirements types, you can do one of the following:
 Leave the Reqmt Type for Indep Reqmts section blank. In Reqmt Type of Customer
Reqmt, enter the copy of the requirements type that links to requirements class
200, 201, or 040 to get a planning strategy similar to MTO production (strategy 20).
 In the Reqmts type for indep. Reqmts section, enter requirements type “VSE”
(which links to requirements class 103). In the Reqmt Type of Customer Reqmt sec-
tion, enter the copy of the requirements type that links to requirements class 045
to get a planning strategy similar to planning without final assembly (strategy 50).
 In the Reqmts Type for Indep. Reqmts section, enter requirements type “VSEV”
(which links to requirements class 104). In the Reqmt Type of Customer Reqmt sec-
tion, enter the copy of the requirements type that links to requirements class 060 to
get a planning strategy similar to planning without planning material (strategy 60).
 In the Reqmts Type for Indep. Reqmts section, enter requirements type “VSE” (which
links to requirements class 103). For Reqmt Type of Customer Reqmt, enter a copy of
the requirements type that links to requirements class 200 to get a planning strat-
egy similar to assembly processing with characteristics planning (strategy 89).

sections.title: Creating a Strategy Group and Including the Newly Created Planning
sections.text:
12.7.4 Creating a Strategy Group and Including the Newly Created Planning
Strategy
In this step, you go to menu path SAP IMG  Production  Master Planning  Demand
Management  Planning Strategy and follow these steps:
1. Select the Define Strategy Group line.
2. Select an existing line (e.g., Strtgy Grp 82).
3. Make a copy and change the Strategy Group key (use a number range starting with
Z or 9).
4. Enter short text in the Description field and enter the new planning strategy in Main
Plg. Straty to link to the newly created planning strategy. Save your settings.

sections.title: Assigning a Strategy Group to a Material Requirements Planning Group
sections.text:
12.7.5 Assigning a Strategy Group to a Material Requirements Planning Group
To assign the strategy group to an MRP group, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production 
Production Planning  Demand Management  Planned Independent Requirement  Plan-
ning Strategy  Assign MRP Group to Strategy Group (see Figure 12.10).
You can now use the planning strategy in the material masters. In the following
sections, we’ll cover how to create various requirements, such as PIRs and customer-
independent requirements, and we’ll discuss these evaluations.

12 Demand Management
656
Figure 12.10 Assignment of Strategy Group to MRP Group and Plant

sections.title: Business Processes in Demand Management
sections.text:
12.8 Business Processes in Demand Management
In the following sections, we’ll show you how to either manually maintain PIRs or copy
the planning figures from one of the several planning tools available, such as forecast-
ing. We then cover customer independent requirements and evaluate all require-
ments. Finally, we’ll show you how to reorganize or delete PIRs that are either too old
or no longer needed for planning purposes.

sections.title: Planned Independent Requirements
sections.text:
12.8.1 Planned Independent Requirements
PIRs are the direct outcome of S&OP, and they form an integral part of demand man-
agement. During the MRP run, the system refers to PIRs with an active version to come
up with procurement proposals.
Note
Refer to Chapter 11 for information about S&OP and how data and information flow
into demand management.
You can create PIRs independently. In fact, demand management offers a far greater
number of options to create PIRs than S&OP does. To create PIRs, follow menu path
Logistics  Production  Production Planning  Demand Management  Planned Indepen-
dent Requirements  Create or use Transaction MD61.
Figure 12.11 shows the initial view of the screen where you create PIRs. For this example,
enter Material “2264” and Plant “1710” and also assign enter “00” (Requirements Plan)
as the Version.
Next, define the planning period in months so that the system brings up the relevant
field for data input. Notice that you can also enter default user parameters by choosing
the User Parameters button and then entering the relevant details. These parameters
then form the default settings for PIRs. However, if you preset a requirements type, it
overrules the strategy settings, so we recommend that you don’t do that.
© 

657
12.8 Business Processes in Demand Management
Figure 12.11 Initial Screen of Planned Independent Requirements
Then, press (Enter), and the PIR view will appear. In it, you can manually enter monthly
production figures for Version 00 (see Figure 12.12). Notice the checkbox for column A,
which indicates that the PIR for material 2264, plant 1710, and version 00 is active. An
active version indicates that the system will consider it during the operational plan-
ning run (in MRP). This compares with pMRP, in which the system can also consider an
inactive version to come up with procurement and production proposals (which are
simulated).
Figure 12.12 PIRs
Figure 12.13 shows the Items tab of the PIR for material 2264, wherein the system
assigns the requirement types of PIRs based on the strategy type defined in the mate-
rial master.

12 Demand Management
658
Figure 12.13 Consumption Type in PIRs
For this example, the system assigns requirement type VSF, which is for planning strat-
egy 40. Pressing (F4) while keeping the cursor in the CI (consumption indicator) field
reveals that Consumption 1 to Consume Only with Customer Requirements applies.
Figure 12.14 shows the detailed Schedule Lines view of the PIRs. Because you chose a
monthly planning period on the initial PIRs view, you can change it, if needed. The sys-
tem also shows the planning (financial) values of the quantities. However, there are
other options available to change the time granularity to daily or weekly.
Figure 12.14 Schedule Lines in PIRs with Financial Values
© 

659
12.8 Business Processes in Demand Management
Note
Using Transaction MD61, you can see the history of changes for a PI, but you won’t be
able to view the PIR reduction. However, you can activate the history during PIR reduc-
tion in configuration Transaction OMP4. The PIR reduction history will always appear
under user ABBAU-, which is an internal identification, not the user who actually
posted the goods movement.
In addition to the option to manually enter the production quantities in the view
shown earlier in Figure 12.12, there are options to automatically copy PIRs from several
other planning tools. These are shown in Figure 12.15, where a planner can copy the
results from the forecast of the material (the forecast of the material must already be
executed and the results saved), from a sales plan, or from a production plan. They can
even copy results from simulative planning, such as pMRP.
Figure 12.15 Options Available to Create PIRs
Note
Chapter 15 discusses pMRP in detail.
Another highly intuitive feature of PIR creation is that you can also compare the PIR
quantities with consumption, forecasting results, and sales or production plans. To do
this, you choose menu path More  Edit  Compare Reqs With.

12 Demand Management
660
When you’ve entered the quantities into the PIR or transferred them to S&OP, you need
to assign a version number, which denotes different demand plans. There may be
several inactive versions and even some simulation versions available in the system.
The inactive or simulation versions are used in a simulation tool, such as pMRP, whereas
an active version becomes part of operative planning (like MRP). We recommend that for
the SAP S/4HANA system, you have version 00 as the one and only active version while
either keeping all other versions inactive or using them in simulations (like pMRP).
Although the standard version numbers available are generally sufficient for the busi-
ness processes in a company, you can create new version numbers if you need to or
amend the existing ones to make them more reflective of your business needs. To cre-
ate a new version, use menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning  Demand Manage-
ment  Planned Independent Requirements  Define Version Numbers (see Figure 12.16)
or use Transaction OMP2.
Apart from creating and changing PIRs in SAP GUI transactions, you can use SAP
S/4HANA to manage a demand plan in an SAP Fiori application called Maintain PIRs
(see Figure 12.17).
When you’re using this application, a list of materials with KPIs related to the PIRs will
appear. Here, you can view key figures, as well as the accuracy during the current
period, last week, and last month. In this context, accuracy is the percentage of PIRs
consumed/reduced by actual demand.
Figure 12.16 Versions for PIRs
When you’re selecting one or more materials, the Edit option allows you to change the
PIRs, and the Upload and Edit option allows you to upload a CSV file with the PIR quan-
tities and then change the uploaded values.
© 

661
12.8 Business Processes in Demand Management
Figure 12.17 Manage PIRs App
If you choose the option to upload a file, a popup screen will appear, as shown in Figure
12.18 1. On that screen, you can choose the file you want to upload. This option to
upload PIRs is a major innovation of this new SAP Fiori application. It wasn’t available
in the previous application, and there’s no simple feature to upload PIRs in SAP GUI.
Either after the file upload or if you simply chose the Edit option, you’ll see a new
option to change the existing or the newly uploaded PIR quantities.
Figure 12.18 Uploading PIRs from Manage PIRs App

12 Demand Management
662
As you can see 2, this screen will be very similar to Transaction MD62 in SAP GUI, in
which there are different materials and PIR quantities in the period buckets. In the
upper-right corner, there’s a Mass Maintenance option, in which you can set a value for
a given period for all the materials displayed. You can also use the Download to Spread-
sheet option (located right next to Mass Maintenance) to download a demand plan as
a CSV file 3. You can use this CSV file as a model for uploading PIRs, as shown earlier.

sections.title: Customer-Independent Requirements
sections.text:
12.8.2 Customer-Independent Requirements
When a user enters a sales order into the system, the system immediately adjusts it
with the PIR as consumption. While sales orders and associated activities, such as out-
bound delivery and posting GI, form the basis of consumption and PIR adjustments,
it’s also possible to independently create customer-independent requirements in
demand management without reference to a sales order.
To create customer-independent requirements, you follow menu path Logistics  Pro-
duction  Production Planning  Demand Management  Customer Requirements  Cre-
ate or use Transaction MD81. Figure 12.19 shows the initial view in which you can create
the independent requirement.
Figure 12.19 Creation of Independent Requirements
© 

663
12.8 Business Processes in Demand Management
When you drill down in the Requirements type field (by pressing (F4) while keeping the
cursor on the field), the system reveals a large number of standard requirement types
to choose from that are specific to the customer. If you’ve assigned a strategy group in
the MRP 3 view of the material master, the system automatically assigns a requirement
class to the customer-independent requirements.
Note
While the system automatically fills in the Requirements type field whenever you
assign a strategy group to the material master, you can overwrite what’s in the field if
you’ve defined alternative strategies in the strategy group.
Press (Enter) and the screen in Figure 12.20 appears. Here, you enter Material number
“2264” and an Order Quantity of “100” for Plnt “1710.”. Then, save the customer inde-
pendent requirements, and the system will generate an internal number for the cus-
tomer requirement, which in this example is 50000008.
Figure 12.20 Independent Requirements with Requirement Type KSV
Next, run the MRP to see how the system accounts for the customer requirements in
the planning run.

sections.title: Planning and Stock/Requirements List for Independent Requirements
sections.text:
12.8.3 Planning and Stock/Requirements List for Independent Requirements
To plan for the independent requirement, run the MRP on material 2264 and plant 1710
by using Transaction MD01N.
To evaluate the stock/requirements list, including independent requirements after the
MRP run, you use the menu path Logistics  Production  Production Planning  Demand
Management  Environment  Stock/Requirements List or use Transaction MD04. As
shown in Figure 12.21, the customer requirement is shown as CusReq in the MRP Ele-
ment column.

12 Demand Management
664
Figure 12.21 Results of MRP with Customer Requirements

sections.title: Total Independent Requirements: Evaluation
sections.text:
12.8.4 Total Independent Requirements: Evaluation
At any given time, you can evaluate the total PIRs of a material and its consumption.
The system displays the Planned Qty. as available in the system, whereas the Total
Assignment column shows the consumption figures. Use menu path Logistics  Produc-
tion  Production Planning  Demand Management  Evaluations  Display Total Reqmts
or use Transaction MD73. Figure 12.22 shows the initial view. Here, you enter Material
“2264,” enter Plant “1710,” and select the Output List 1 option from the dropdown list.
Press (Enter) and the Display Ind Requirements with Assigned Cust. Requirements
view shown in Figure 12.23 will appear. Here, the monthly planned quantity previously
entered is visible in the upper half of the view, along with the customer requirement.
When a planner has maintained only the dynamic consumption, then this is shown in
the Total Assgmnt column. If the entire process is finished—that is, if the sales order
has been delivered—then the system shows the quantity in the Withdrawal Qty col-
umn.
© 

665
12.8 Business Processes in Demand Management
Figure 12.22 Initial Screen for Total Requirements of Material 2264
Figure 12.23 Total PIR and Requirements for Material 2264

12 Demand Management
666

sections.title: Total Independent Requirements: Reorganization
sections.text:
12.8.5 Total Independent Requirements: Reorganization
Although the PIRs are automatically reduced, a planner must regularly and periodically
evaluate the PIRs in the system to see if they need to be eliminated (or adjusted). This
helps ensure that old and irrelevant planning data no longer becomes part of the plan-
ning process during the MRP run.
To evaluate the PIR, you use menu path Logistics  Production  Production Planning 
Demand Management  Environment  Independent Requirements: Reorganization 
Adjust Requirements or use Transaction MD74. In the initial view, you maintain the ini-
tial parameters so that when the system is executed, it only brings up the relevant results
for consideration and action. Notice that you have the flexibility to evaluate the results of
the organization in Test Mode (No database changes) (see Figure 12.24). This lets you
evaluate the results in simulative mode before performing actual reorganization.
Figure 12.24 Initial Screen for Reorganizing PIRs
The Key Date field refers to the date until which the system should bring up the PIR data
for reorganization. For this example, if 07/01/2025 (July 01, 2025) is maintained, then
the system should consider all of the PIRs until the month of July in 2025. It’s important
to highlight that such PIR reorganization is only ensured for past and old PIRs, ones the
© 

667
12.8 Business Processes in Demand Management
company no longer wants to plan or account for in demand management. The Rel. Key
Date field specifies the relative key date, with reference to today, for which the
demands will be processed. For example, you enter if −200 days, then the system
selects and processes all demands that lie 200 days in the past and earlier.
Tips and Tricks
To define the plant-level PIR reorganization, you use Transaction OMP8, wherein you

12.8.6 Planned Independent Requirements: Reduction
PIRs are reduced during GI, and unlike during consumption, the PIRs are actually
reduced and not just consumed. During MTS production using strategy 10, the oldest
PIR is reduced first. However, the reduction can also affect PIRs in the future if relevant

12 Demand Management
668
strategies are used with consumption, such as reduction in consumption strategies 40,
50, 60, and so on.
Here, reduction simply takes place according to the current consumption situation
when you post the GI. For this strategy to work, you need to set the configuration data
accordingly. You perform the configuration using Transaction OMJJ. For nonconsump-
tion strategy 10, this setting is available in the requirements class. This transaction is in
the domain of MM, wherein you make relevant settings per movement type. For non-
consumption strategy 10, these settings are available in the requirements class.
After executing Transaction OMJJ, you provide the movement type—for example,
“601” (GI against delivery)—in the Updating Control area, as shown in Figure 12.26.
Check the Ind. Rqmts Reduction box to enable the system to reduce the PIRs at the time
of GI (e.g., movement type 601) against a sales order. During assembly planning, the PIR
is reduced when goods are withdrawn for the production order.

sections.title: Summary
sections.text:
12.9 Summary
12.9 Summary
Demand management acts as a central point to account for both planning for produc-
tion processes and consumption for sales processes. If you use the right strategy type
for each material based on its production process, demand management can optimize
business processes not only by helping you enter realistic planning quantities such as
PIRs but also by showing you how they relate to the automatic and dynamic adjust-
ment of PIRs upon receipt of sales orders and their further processing, including GI. The
flexibility to assign different planning and consumption strategies broadly mitigates
the insecurities and other risks involved. At the same time, the system keeps a realistic
check on the quantities entered in PIRs compared to the actual demand situation by
making appropriate adjustments.
The next chapter covers MRP.
